This means that in addition to paying a dividend yield that exceeds the 4% rule that many investors target for retirement, it is also expected to grow that payout at a rate that beats the Fed's current rate of inflation and is about double what the Fed's target rate of inflation is over that period of time. Therefore, it checks all the boxes for someone looking to retire on dividends in a very sustainable manner.

The biggest risks facing O are:

  1. It has gotten so large that it is getting harder and harder to sustain its growth rate and its quality. For now, it has been able to make some large, needle-moving acquisitions to drive growth, and the triple net lease real estate market remains fragmented enough to give it adequate opportunities to achieve growth, but this will likely eventually become a significant issue for the REIT.
  2. It remains very sensitive to long-term interest rates due to the bond-like nature of its cash flow and the fact that it uses debt along with equity to make acquisitions and has to regularly refinance existing debt.

Enbridge: A Utility-Like Energy Giant

Another opportunity that I like a lot right now is Enbridge (ENB). It is a midstream energy infrastructure company (UTF) that has virtually all of its cash flow coming from either long-duration contracts or regulated assets, making it a utility-like investment. Moreover, ENB has very little cash flow exposure to commodity price volatility.

Additionally, it pays out a 5.5% dividend yield, putting it in a similar category as O when it comes to dividend yield. Its expected distributable cash flow per share CAGR is 5.2% through the end of the decade, once again positioning it to grow its dividend at a pace that comfortably exceeds both the current rate of inflation and the Fed's target rate of inflation.

With a BBB+ credit rating reflecting its solid balance sheet, well-laddered debt maturities, and very stable cash flow profile, ENB is resilient against both recession and commodity price swings. Its assets are diversified across geographies and commodities, including substantial exposure to natural gas (UNG), which positions it well to thrive in the age of AI while also being a very defensive play against economic turmoil. This makes it an ideal investment for retirement.

That being said, ENB is not risk-free. In particular, it has considerable regulatory and execution risk since it is regularly investing in major growth projects that require successful construction as well as regulatory approvals. Additionally, it has some secondary exposure to energy commodity pricing since it services businesses that are very energy price sensitive. Finally, its long duration, bond-like cash flows, and significant leverage make it sensitive to long-term interest rates in a manner similar to how O is.

SCHD: A Dividend Growth Powerhouse

Last but not least, this portfolio can be rounded out nicely with the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). It has a very low expense ratio of just 0.06%, a forward dividend yield of about 4%, and over 100 blue-chip holdings diversified across numerous sectors, including energy, consumer defensive, healthcare, industrials, consumer cyclical, technology, financials, and communication stocks.

While it lacks much in the way of exposure to utilities, infrastructure, and real estate-which make O and ENB particularly attractive complements to it since they slot nicely into these sectors-its greatest attribute is its strong track record of dividend growth. SCHD has grown its dividend every year for 13 straight years, complementing ENB and O's dividend aristocrat-like 25+ year streaks. With a 10.43% dividend CAGR over the past decade, it is a powerful dividend growth machine that can further accelerate the combined dividend growth of the three dividend investments mentioned in this article.

The biggest downside/risk of investing in SCHD is that it lacks exposure to mega-cap technology stocks and is heavily invested in energy stocks (XLE) after its recent portfolio reconstitution. This means that during the recent AI boom that has driven mega-cap tech stocks higher, SCHD has lagged the broader market (SPY) quite significantly. Additionally, given that energy stocks can be quite volatile, SCHD could also experience short-term price volatility.

Investor Takeaway

Between O, ENB, and SCHD, an investor can have a pretty well-diversified portfolio through a real estate giant, an infrastructure giant, and a broadly diversified low-cost ETF that owns some of the leading dividend growth companies out there. If combined in a portfolio, these three stocks in aggregate would likely yield well in excess of 4% and grow their aggregate payout at a rate that easily beats inflation over time while having strong underlying balance sheets and business models.

While I do not follow this exact approach and instead select individual stocks and implement an opportunistic capital recycling program to achieve higher yields, similar dividend growth rate, and higher total returns, the philosophy underpinning these three dividend investment options is very similar to what I implement in my retirement portfolio as a high-yield investor at High Yield Investor.
